summary: This notebook demonstrates a comprehensive baseline for NCAA basketball tournament prediction by combining traditional seeding, Elo ratings, and GLM-derived team quality metrics. It walks through data preparation, including overtime adjustments and symmetric home/away feature creation, followed by extensive feature engineering. The author trains leave-one-season-out XGBoost models to predict point differentials, then applies spline-based calibration to convert margins into win probabilities. Readers should learn how to construct robust temporal features, calibrate regression outputs for classification tasks, and evaluate predictor stability across seasons.
competition_flow: Loads NCAA basketball CSVs, computes Elo and GLM-based team quality metrics, trains leave-one-season-out XGBoost models on point differentials, calibrates predictions with splines, and averages fold predictions to generate a submission.
data_reading: Reads multiple CSVs (MRegularSeasonDetailedResults.csv, MNCAATourneyDetailedResults.csv, MNCAATourneySeeds.csv, etc.) using pd.read_csv.
Concatenates men's and women's datasets and filters rows by a minimum season year (>= 2003).
data_processing: Adjusts overtime stats by dividing by (40 + 5 * NumOT) / 40 to normalize accumulated statistics.
Creates symmetric T1/T2 features by swapping home/away roles and concatenating.
Calculates point differentials, merges seed/Elo/quality data, clips predictions to [-30, 30], and applies spline calibration.
features_engineering: Season averages for box score stats (FGM, FGA, OR, DR, Blk, PF, etc.) for both teams and opponents.
Elo ratings calculated per season using standard win/loss update rules.
GLM-based team quality scores using fixed-effects regression on point differentials.
Differences in seeds, Elo, and quality; men/women indicator.
cv_strategy: Leave-one-season-out cross-validation (trains on all seasons except one, validates on the held-out season)
ensembling: Averages predictions from leave-one-season-out models and applies manual alpha blending for extreme probabilities (>0.9 or <0.1).
insights: GLM-derived team quality consistently outperforms seed difference as a predictor across multiple seasons.
Spline calibration significantly improves probability calibration (Brier score) over raw point differentials.
Overtime accumulation skews raw stats, requiring normalization to prevent feature leakage.
Symmetric feature engineering doubles the effective training data and stabilizes model inputs.
critical_findings: Raw overtime statistics accumulate disproportionately, necessitating a specific adjustment factor.
Seed difference remains a surprisingly strong baseline, but GLM quality surpasses it in most years.
Several box score metrics were commented out, suggesting they may not add predictive value or were omitted for brevity.
